coredata.dc:title: A melanin-integrated graphene quantum dots embedded molecularly imprinted polymer: A nano-fluorescence sensing probe for trace determination of prometryn
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Chumkaeo, Peerapong
Davis, Frank
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A nanocomposite fluorescence sensing probe was successfully designed and utilized for the ultrasensitive and highly selective determination of prometryn. The nanoprobe incorporated melanin nanoparticles, as an affinity material, and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as fluorophores in molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP). Multiple techniques were employed to characterize the synthesized essential precursors and MIP fluorescent nanoprobe. The developed nanoprobe was then applied to detect prometryn, demonstrating linearity from 1.0 to 100.0 μg kg−1 with a limit of detection of 0.63 μg kg−1. When used to determine prometryn in real samples, the developed nanoprobe achieved recoveries from 92.4 % to 102.8 % with RSDs less than 5.4 %. The simplicity, reliability, selectivity and sensitivity of the proposed sensor indicated that the combination of MIP and eco-friendly materials produced an excellent fluorescent sensor for the detection of trace analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prometryn
Fluorescent probe
Graphene quantum dots
Melanin
Molecularly imprinted polymer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Characterization of the composite nano-fluorescence probe and related materials
Effect of pH of dispersion solution
Effect of mole ratio of template-to-monomer-to-cross linker
Comparison of different nano-fluorescence sensing probes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Postdoctoral Fellowship from Prince of Songkla University. The authors acknowledge the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanocomposite probe for the determination of prometryn.

TEM image of melanin nanoparticles (A) and their particle size distribution histogram (B), SEM image of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobes (C) and particle size distribution histogram (D), XPS survey spectra of N-GQDs (E), C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum of N-GQDs (F), O 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum of N-GQDs (G) and N 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum of N-GQDs (H).
The effect of binding time between prometryn and composite fluorescence sensing probe (A), pH of dispersion solution (B), template to monomer to cross-linker (C) and melanin loading (D).
Sensitivity of different sensing probes towards the determination of prometryn (n = 3).

Fluorescence spectra of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP (A), melanin@N-GQDs@NIP (B) in the present of different concentration of prometryn, calibration curve (C) and photograph of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP in the present (right) and absent (left) of prometryn.

HPLC chromatogram of real sample and spiked sample at various concentration (a; 0, b = 0.10; c = 0.50; d = 0.75; e = 1.0 mg L−1) (A) and comparison of the developed method and HPLC method for the determination of prometryn (B).
Analytical results of prometryn in real samples using the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobe (n = 3).
Comparison of analytical performances of the developed method and other methods for the determination of prometryn.

head.ce:title: A melanin-integrated graphene quantum dots embedded molecularly imprinted polymer: A nano-fluorescence sensing probe for trace determination of prometryn

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanocomposite fluorescence sensing probe was successfully designed and utilized for the ultrasensitive and highly selective determination of prometryn. The nanoprobe incorporated melanin nanoparticles, as an affinity material, and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as fluorophores in molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP). Multiple techniques were employed to characterize the synthesized essential precursors and MIP fluorescent nanoprobe. The developed nanoprobe was then applied to detect prometryn, demonstrating linearity from 1.0 to 100.0 μg kg−1 with a limit of detection of 0.63 μg kg−1. When used to determine prometryn in real samples, the developed nanoprobe achieved recoveries from 92.4 % to 102.8 % with RSDs less than 5.4 %. The simplicity, reliability, selectivity and sensitivity of the proposed sensor indicated that the combination of MIP and eco-friendly materials produced an excellent fluorescent sensor for the detection of trace analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Herbicides are widely present in the environment and our foods. Their presence endangers human health due to their acute and chronic toxicity, reproductive and developmental toxicity, genotoxicity and carcinogenicity [1]. One of the most extensively used symmetrical triazine herbicides is prometryn, which poses hazards to human health at high dosages and by its protracted residual period [2]. Consequently, it is important to determine the quantities of prometryn in biological samples and dietary products. Several qualitative and quantitative methods have been developed to determine prometryn. The methods used include chemiluminescence [3], electrochemiluminescence [4], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [5], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [6], optical sensors [7], lateral flow immunochromatographic strips [8] and high-performance liquid chromatography [9]. However, these methods are expensive, time-consuming and require expert operators. To avoid these limitations, fluorescence sensors are an interesting alternative method due to their outstanding sensitivity, quick response times and technological versatility. Moreover, the sensitivity of fluorescence sensors can be enhanced using composite selective nano-sized sensing probes containing high fluorescence sensing materials.

Due to their low cytotoxicity, high water dispersibility, high photostability, strong biocompatibility, unique electrical properties, low preparation cost and fluorescence features [10], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered increased attention in sensing applications. In this respect, nitrogen (N) doping is helpful in modifying the intrinsic features of GQDs [11]. N-GQDs are widely utilized as sensing probes for the detection of metal cations, small organic compounds, non-metal and biological macromolecules [12]. The synthesis of N-GQDs has been carried out using a variety of techniques, including electrochemical [13], chemical vapor deposition [14], laser ablation in liquid (LAL) [15], hydrothermal [16] and oxidative ultrasonication [17]. The hydrothermal synthesis of N-GQDs is the most widely used, straightforward and affordable technique [18]. Nevertheless, for analytical application, analogous structural compounds and other matrix components can interfere with the accurate identification of target analytes in complex samples employing N-GQDs. To mitigate this, N-GQDs can be combined with highly selective materials to form a nanohybrid composite sensing probe with enhanced selectivity.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are widely recognized as a promising class of high-tech functional materials. Depending on the functionality and stereochemistry of the target molecule or structural analogue, appropriate recognition cavities are “printed” within a 3D structure to create a new class of tailor-made materials [19]. Molecular imprinting is an effective technique for preparing molecular recognition materials, exhibiting potential utilization prospects in broadly applicable areas such as the stationary phase of chromatography [20], solid-phase extraction (SPE) materials [21], simulating enzymaticreactions [22], heterogeneous catalysis [23], chemical sensors [24] and membrane separation techniques [25]. The imprinting process involves the polymerization of functional monomers and a cross-linker around a template molecule. The subsequent removal of the template leaves binding sites within the polymer network. It is essential to note that the imprinted sites contain a 3D network containing cavities within which the geometry and position of various polymer functional groups exist in a manner which are complementary to the template molecule after it is removed.

To improve the adsorption ability of the nano-fluorescence probe, melanin is an innocuous, naturally occurring biopolymer with a wide distribution in diverse biosystems. Melanin is produced by a complex biochemical pathway that involves several enzymes [26] and intermediate molecules [27]. Melanin has shown promise as a biocompatible and biodegradable material for use in biomedical applications, such as drug delivery [28], tissue engineering [29], and imaging [30]. The synthesis and application of melanin in chemical research is an area of growing interest and holds great potential for a wide range of applications.

In this work, a fluorescent nanoprobe was fabricated to determine trace levels of prometryn. The nanoprobe is composed of melanin nanoparticles and N-GQDs embedded in a MIP (melanin@N-GQDs@MIP). Melanin can help improve the adsorption of prometryn on the nanoprobe. N-GQDs can improve the sensitivity of response and the MIP increases selectivity. These materials were chosen to produce a rapid, highly sensitive and selective nanoprobe to determine prometryn in food samples. The results obtained with the proposed sensor were compared with the results obtained with a chromatographic technique.

Prometryn, cyanazine, dimethametryn, terbuthylazine and terbutryn were purchased from Dr. Ehrenstorfer GmbH (Augsburg, Germany). Citric acid, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris-HMA) and dopamine hydrochloride were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Germany). Di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) were from Ajax Chemical Ltd. (Australia). Ammonia and ethanol were from Labscan (Thailand). Stock herbicide solutions were prepared at 1,000 mg L−1 in ethanol and stored at −4 °C. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.cm at 25 °C was utilized throughout.

Transmission electron microscopy was performed using a JEOL JEM-2010 microscope (Japan). The accelerating voltage was 120 kV. The samples of MIP nanoprobes were prepared by ultrasonically dispersing the samples in ultrapure water. The dispersions of the sample were dropped and deposited on a formvar-coated Cu grid with the solvent evaporated under ambient conditions. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was performed using a JSM-5200 scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The samples were examined under high vacuum and 15 kV accelerating voltage. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was employed to examine the chemical composition of the sample surface. (XPS; AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos analytical, Manchester UK). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis chamber was maintained at an initial pressure of approximately 5 × 10−9 torr. The specimens were energized using a 700 × 300 µm spot area X-ray hybrid mode, employing monochromatic Al Kα 1, 2 radiation at 1.4 keV. The X-ray anode was operated at a voltage of 15 kV, a current of 10 mA and a power output of 150 W. The hemispherical analyser was utilized to detect the photoelectrons which were positioned at an angle of 45° relative to the sample surface's normal. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the powdered catalyst samples were measured at −196 °C using a Quantachrome Instruments v11.0 instrument. The specific surface areas were calculated by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method. Prior to these measurements, the samples were outgassed at 200 °C for 12 h. Fluorescence spectra were performed on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer from Agilent (California, United States). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by an Avaspec 2048 spectrometer (Apeldoorn, the Netherlands). FT-IR spectra were recorded by the diffuse reflectance method performed using a BX FT-IR spectroscope (PerkinElmer, MA, USA) with KBr pellets as diluents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were prepared by heating 2.00 g of citric acid and 0.50 g of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane in a 25.0 mL round-bottom flask at 225 °C for 15 min. Transparent molten citric acid was formed after around 3 min and the colourless molten liquid turned orange after 8 min and was cooled to room temperature. After dissolving the substance in 5.00 mL of ultrapure water, a dropwise addition of 1.0 M NaOH solution brought the pH of the solution to 7.0. The salt ions in the solution were then removed using a dialysis bag (with a retained molecular weight of 1000 Da) for 12 h to result in a pale-yellow N-GQDs solution and completely eliminating salt ions. Before characterization, N-GQDs were dried and examined in the solid state. For freeze-drying, the dialysed N-GQDs solution was then quickly frozen in liquid nitrogen and transferred to a freezer dryer for lyophilization.

Melanin nanoparticles were synthesized from dopamine hydrochloride according to a reported method [27]. A dopamine hydrochloride solution was prepared by dissolving 180 mg of dopamine hydrochloride in 90.0 mL of DI water. The solution was then heated to 50 °C and 760.0 μLof 1.0 M NaOH solution was added while the mixture was being vigorously stirred. The clear solution turned light yellow to darkbrown. After the reaction was completed, the obtained melanin precipitate was washed three times with DIwater by centrifugation at 18000 rpm to remove unreacted dopamine hydrochloride, salts and oligomers. The collected precipitate was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for three times to remove the larger-sized materials and the collected supernatant was centrifuged at 18000 rpm. Finally, melanin nanoparticles were collected and dried at 80 °C.

The melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobe was synthesized according to the following procedure. Prometryn (6.0 mg) and APTES (48 μL) were dissolved in 5.0 mL ethanol and stirred for 1 h at room temperature. Then 5.0 mL of synthesized N-GQD solution (obtained from Section 2.3) and 2.0 mg of synthesized melanin were added into the mixture solution and stirred for 1 h. Following this 400.0 μL of TEOS and 150.0 μL of 25 % w/v NH3 were added and the mixture stirred for 4 h. After polymerization, the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol three times, and dried at 60 °C. A non-imprinted polymer nanoprobe (melanin@N-GQDs@NIP) was also synthesized using the same procedure but without adding the prometryn (template). The synthesized procedure of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanocomposites probe is demonstrated in Fig. 1 .

Fluorescence measurements were performed according to the following strategy. A suspension of synthesized nano-fluorescence probes was prepared by dispersion of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobe in potassium-phosphate buffer pH 7.0. Then, 300.0 μL of the dispersion solution was mixed with 100.0 μL of prometryn standard or sample solution, and the mixture was incubated for 16 min which is the optimum binding time. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture solution was measured and recorded from 335 to 700 nm by fixing an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The slit width was fixed at 10 nm and the measurement was conducted at room temperature (25 °C).

The samples were blended into a homogeneous mixture. The analyte was extracted from homogenised fruit samples according to the following procedure. 15.0 g of sample homogenate was extracted with 10.0 mL of acetonitrile and then combined with 1.00 g of anhydrous MgSO4 and 1.00 g of NaCl to eliminate excess water. The sample was sonicated for 40 min and centrifuged. Then, the supernatant was collected, evaporated at 50 °C to remove extraction solvent (acetonitrile), and redissolved in DI water (10.0 mL).

HPLC analysis was performed using a Hewlett-Packard 1100 series HPLC system (Agilent Technologies, Germany). A Fortis C18 column (4.6 mm × 150 mm, 5 µm) was utilized for the chromatographic separation of the target analyte. The mobile phase was 50:50 (v/v) ultrapure water (A) and acetonitrile (B). The mobile phase was filtratedthrough a 0.22 µm membrane filter. The detection wavelength was 220 nm and column temperature was maintained at 25 °C. The flow rate of the mobile phase was controlled at 1.00 mL min−1; the injection volume was 20.0 μL.
The fabricated nanoprobe was characterized and the fabrication strategy and detection condition were investigated and optimized to achieve the optimal sensitivity with a short determination time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the composite nano-fluorescence probe and related materials
ce:section.ce:para: A TEM image of the synthesized melanin nanoparticles is displayed in Fig. 2 A. The synthesized melanin nanoparticles were evenly distributed in a spherical form with a small distribution of size and a diameter of around 144 nm (Fig. 2B). To validate additionally the particle size of melanin nanoparticles, the hydrodynamic diameter of nanoparticles was evaluated using the Zetasizer Nano ZS dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument. The DLS results indicated that the hydrodynamic particle size of the melanin nanoparticles was 143.8 nm. Clearly, the particle sizes measured by DLS and TEM corresponded adequately with the estimated sizes.

Melanin@N-GQDs@MIP and melanin@N-GQDs@NIP were both synthesized and characterized. After the polymers were synthesized and the template was removed, an SEM was performed to examine the morphology of the polymers. The SEM image displays polymer particles with consistent sizes and shapes (Fig. 2C). The synthesized nanocomposite probes were spherical and the average diameter was about 540 nm (Fig. 2D).

To determine the composition of the as-produced N-GQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 2E) of the resultant N-GQDs exhibit a graphitic C 1 s peak at approximately 284 eV and an O 1 s peak at approximately 534 eV. For the resulting N-GQDs, a prominent N 1 s peak was observed. This demonstrated the effective incorporation of N atoms into the structure of GQDs by the method of hydrothermal synthesis using tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane as a source for the nitrogen-feedstock. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of the N-GQDs (Fig. 2F) reveals the existence of three peaks corresponding to C = C/C–C (283.981 eV), C–N (285.618 eV), and C = N/C = O (287.314 eV), respectively. The O 1 s spectrum of N-GDQs has three peaks (532.3 eV) corresponding to C–OH (530.262 eV), C = O (531.523), and C–O–C (532.632 eV) in addition to the aforementioned bond (Fig. 2G). In addition, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of N-GQDs reveals that there are three forms of nitrogen atoms including pyridinic (399.548 eV), pyrrolic (400.901 eV), and graphitic N (402.875 eV) (Fig. 2H). As shown in Fig. 2E, the XPS scanning spectrum reveals the presence of C, O, and N with respective atomic concentrations of 67.7 %, 28.3 %, and 3.92 %.

The FT-IR study was performed on samples of N-GQDs, melanin nanoparticles and melanin@N-GQDs@MIP. An FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs is displayed in Fig. S1(a), A strong broad peak at ∼ 2980–3440 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration mode of N–H and O–H stretching. Absorption bands at 1194, 1541 and 1734 cm−1 corresponded to C–O, N–H and C = O bending respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of melanin nanoparticles is shown in Fig. S1(b), the band at 3386 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH2 groups. The band at 1597 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrational bending of C = C bonds. The peak at 1272 cm−1 correspond to the amine group and the hydroxyl stretching vibration of phenolic compounds. The FT-IR spectrum of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP is displayed in Fig. S1(c), the bands at 3422 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H, indicating the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups. The bands at 1583 cm−1 in the spectrum of the MIP probe correspond to the bending vibrations of N–H groups from the APTES, indicating the presence of aminopropyl groups on the sensor's surface. The broad peak around 1092 cm−1 and the distinctive peaks at 798 and 427 cm−1 are attributed to the Si–O–Si asymmetric and the Si–O vibrations, respectively. These results can confirm that N-GQDs and melanin nanoparticles were successfully incorporated into the MIP matrix to form the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe.

Key functions are played by the template molecules (prometryn), monomer (APTES) and cross-linker (TEOS) in the formation of the porous structure of MIP. During polymerization, phase separation between the porogen and increasing polymer results in the formation of pores. The pores serve as pathways for the template to diffuse within the polymer network during binding and to diffuse out during MIP extraction. Melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobes had a larger surface area and larger pore diameter than melanin@N-GQDs@NIP nanoprobes. Melanin@N-GQDs@MIP has a greater surface area than melanin@N-GQDs@NIP due to the greater amount of template molecule extracted during MIP production, indicating its potential for enhanced prometryn adsorption. Melanin@N-GQDs@MIP and melanin@N-GQDs@NIP N2 sorption isotherms reveal N2 adsorption and desorption of MIP. The nanohybrid composite MIP and NIP nanoprobes exhibit H4-type hysteresis (Fig. S2), indicating presence of a mesoporous structure.

The fluorescence quantum yields of N-GQDs and melanin@N-GQDs@MIP were determined to be 70.02 % and 65.36 %, respectively, using quinine sulphate as a fluorescence standard. The fluorescence quantum yield of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP decreased in comparison to naked N-GQDs. In general, the emission of quantum dots can be suppressed by an electric field generated by single-charge species, such as Si–O groups, hydroxide anions, and ammonium cations, resulting in the reduction of quantum yields in molecularly imprinted polymers. Additionally, the presence of melanin nanoparticles can be counted asa major contributor to the reduction in fluorescence quantum yield.

The fluorescence spectra of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP before and after the removal of prometryn are shown in Fig. S3. After removal of the template (prometryn), the fluorescence intensity was restored to almost the same level as that of the NIP probe which implied the template molecules were completely removed from the nanoprobe making it ready to use for the determination of target analyte in real samples.

To obtain the highest sensitivity with the shortest determination time, the effect of binding time, pH of dispersion solution, mole ratio of template to monomer to cross linker and melanin loading were investigated and optimized. Fluorescence quenching (F0/F) was determined to investigate the binding time, where F0 was fluorescence emission intensity of the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe in the absent of prometryn and F was fluorescence intensity of nanoprobe in the presence of prometryn. Also, the sensitivity was evaluated to optimize other parameters. The sensitivity was the slope of the calibration curve obtained by determining prometryn over a range of concentrations (1.0–100 µg/L).

The incubation time for the binding between the analyte (prometryn) and melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe was investigated to maximize the amount of binding within the shortest time. After adding prometryn to a suspension of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP, the fluorescence quenching of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP rose dramatically between 8 min and 22 min (Fig. 3 A) and then remained steady. In subsequent experiments, the incubation period of 22 min was adopted as the incubationtime for the determination of prometryn using the developed nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of dispersion solution
ce:section.ce:para: Melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence intensity towards prometryn in anaqueous medium relies on a variety of interactions. Hydrogen bonding, dipole–dipole interaction, and π-π interaction are included. The effect of pH on the prometryn quenching ability of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, the quenching of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP fluorescent nanoprobes in the pH range from 4.0 to 9.0 was studied. The fluorescence quenching of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP probe to prometryn rose from pH 4.0 to 7.0 and subsequently dropped from pH 7.0 to 9.0. The phenomenon could be explained according to poor interaction of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP to prometryn at low pH probably due to the protonation of amino groups. Also, the imprinting layer might be ionized in an alkaline environment, which would decrease the interaction between MIP nano-fluorescence probe and prometryn. Therefore, pH 7.0 was utilized for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of mole ratio of template-to-monomer-to-cross linker
ce:section.ce:para: Sol-gel polymerization was employed to fabricatemelanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probes without requiring extra surfactants. APTES interacted with prometryn and N-doped graphene quantum dots through hydrogen bonding to produce prometryn-imprinted polymers which were then hydrolyzed and condensed with TEOS. After association with prometryn molecules, the fluorescence intensity of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP was quenched and was restored after the removal of prometryn. Obviously, the molar ratio of template-functional monomer/cross-linker is essential to the recognition ability of the MIP. To produce the greatest fluorescence response for prometryn, the polymerization conditions were optimized, including the loading of prometryn, and the quantities of TEOS and APTES. Melanin@N-GQDs@MIP with various prometryn/APTES/TEOS molar ratios were synthesized. As seen in Fig. 3C, the nanoprobes derived from 400.0 μL of TEOS exhibited a superior fluorescence response towards prometryn. For fluorogenic detection, the doping ratio of N-GQDs to APTES is also crucial, hence MIPs with varying doping ratios were synthesized. When N-GQDs are present in a high concentration (1:6), the sensitivity is low due to the inability of tiny quantities of monomer to react sufficiently with the template and produce sufficient recognition sites. In contrast, when N-GQDs are present in a modest proportion (1:10), sensitivity is poor due to homogeneous self-condensation caused by an excess of monomer, which results in the production of fewer recognition sites. When 5.0 mL of N-GQDs and 48.0 µL of APTES are employed (1:8), the probe exhibits the maximum sensitivity.

The effect of TEOS, which serves an essential function as a cross-linking agent, was described. After a successful investigation into the ratio of prometryn to TEOS, the optimal sensitivity was reached at a mole ratio of 1:72. With a mole ratio of 1:55, sensitivity was decreased, perhaps because unstable MIP networks were formed due to the presence of inadequate crosslinking. At a concentration of 1:80, the sensitivity was reduced because an excess of cross-linker lead to formation of a highly-rigid network which might disrupt the interaction between the template and monomer resulting in a reduction in the number of suitable recognition sites.

The maximum fluorescence quenching effectiveness of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP was achieved when the molar ratio of prometryn: APTES: TEOS was 1:8:72 and this optimized formulation was applied to prepare nanoprobes for future experiments.

As an affinity material, melanin could enhance the physical characteristics of the sensing probe due to the structure of melanin since conjugated moieties such as dihydroxyindole and indolequinone enable the binding of aromatic structures via stacking or other secondary interactions such as hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interactions. In this context, the possibility is that melanin may offer an extra intermolecular force that might improve the performance of composite sensing probe. Therefore, the amount of melanin used within the formulation was evaluated for the optimization of the sensing probes. The quantity of melanin in the composite nanoprobe melanin@N-GQDs@MIP was evaluated in the range from 1.0 to 4.0 mg. 2.0 mg of melanin provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 3D). The sensitivity decreased at lower melanin loading(<2.0 mg). With larger amounts of melanin, the sensitivity was also diminished due to the obstruction of recognition cavities by an abundance of melanin nanoparticles, resulting in fewer accessible binding sites. Thus, a loading of 2.0 mg of melanin nanoparticles was used for the creation of the hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different nano-fluorescence sensing probes
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of different nano-fluorescence probes including N-GQDs@NIP, melanin@N-GQDs@NIP, N-GQDs@MIP, and melanin@N-GQDs@MIP were assessed to assess their ability to detect prometryn (Fig. 4 ). Due to the absence of specific recognition sites and adsorption materials, the N-GQDs@NIP nano-fluorescence probe exhibited the lowest sensitivity towards prometryn. When melanin nanoparticles were incorporated into the composite nanoprobe, its sensitivity was significantly enhanced. In comparison to the results of using various nanocomposite formulations, the hierarchical melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe exhibited the best sensitivity. Because specialized recognition sites and adsorption materials were present, there was greater and much more rapid adsorption of the analyte whichwas the purpose of the experiment. To identify and measure prometryn in real samples, the nanocomposite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP was utilized.

Upon addition of prometryn, there is a discernible drop in the fluorescence intensity measured at 406 nm for the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP, as seen in Fig. 5 . The analytical capabilities of the hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescent probe were evaluated prior to use for the determination of prometryn in real samples. The fluorescence emission spectra of the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP and melanin@N-GQDs@NIP nano-fluorescence probe were recorded in the presence of prometryn at concentrations ranging from 0.0 to 100.0 μg L−1. The hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP probe showed a noticeable drop in emission response with increasing prometryn, but the melanin@N-GQDs@NIP probe only showed a modest decrease in emission response at the equivalent prometryn concentrations (Fig. 5A and B). Due to there being no specific recognition sites in the hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@NIP, only non-specific adsorption took place. As a result, the decrease in fluorescence quenching intensity was less. The target analyte was able to bind with specific recognition sites of the hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP, however there were no recognition sites in the hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@NIP. In the concentration range of 1.0 to 100.0 μg·L−1, the hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobe demonstrated excellent linearity (Fig. 5C). The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) were 0.64 μg L−1 and 2.2 μg L−1, based on 3SD/Slope and 10SD/Slope, respectively. The equation for this relationship is y = (0.0319 ± 0.0005)X[Prometryn]+(1.343 ± 0.043), and its correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.9953. The maximum residue limits (MRLs) specified by the EU are far higher than these values. The European Union (EU) has set the MRL of the total amount of multiple triazine herbicides to 50 μg kg−1 . The image shown in Fig. 5D is a photograph that was taken under UV light and depicts the composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP in both the presence and absence of prometryn. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation [31]. This equation states that.
F0/F = 1 + Ksv[CPrometryn]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence emission intensity of the hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobe in the absence and presence of prometryn, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant for prometryn, and [CPrometryn] is the concentration of the quencher (prometryn). The hierarchical composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobe exhibited a linear Stern-Volmer relationship, which suggested a dynamic quenching process driven by electron transfer [12]. The electrons of the N-GQDs were excited from their ground state to their excited state. As there was no prometryn surrounding the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe, the excited electron to the valence band, which results in the production of fluorescence. On the other hand, when prometryn is present, the amino group (NH2) groups of the monomer (APTES) have the potential to engage with prometryn and create a Meisenheimer complex by the process of hydrogen bonding. The interaction would lead to electron transfer from N-GQDs to prometryn, which would result in the fluorescence being quenched. In addition, the emission spectra of the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe did not coincide with the experimental result that was based on the UV absorption spectrum of prometryn (Fig. S4). As a result, the transfer of energy was ruled out as a potential mechanism for quenching [24]. Therefore, the quenching mechanism was an electron transfer that occurred when prometryn bound with the specific recognition sites of the nanoprobe. In addition, the specific property of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP and melanin@N-GQDs@NIP towards prometryn was estimated using the imprinting factor (IF). KSV(MIPs) and KSV(NIPs) were the linear slopes of MIPs and NIPs, respectively; the IF was defined as the ratio of these two quantities. Under the optimized conditions, the IF value was calculated to be 7.78, which indicated that the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP had outstanding specificity for prometryn sensing.

The selectivity of the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe was investigated by comparing the sensitivity for the determination of prometryn and other similar structured compounds. The structura analogues including cyanazine (CYN), dimethametryn (Dimepax), terbuthylazine (TERB) and terbutryn (TBY), which were structurally related to prometryn were investigated (Fig. S5A). The selectivity factors for CYN, Dimepax, TERB, and TBY were 13.58, 14.82, 14.80, and 10.19, respectively. Since only PY is capable of structural matching with the empty cavities of the MIPs, it is clear that they exert a significant influence on MIP fluorescence and contribute significantly to the sensor's overall selectivity factor. Methylthio-s-triazines are a type of chemical compound found in TBY and Dimepax. Similar to the template molecule, but with less fluorescence quenching since the recognition sites of the imprinted cavities are not analogous to it, these aromatic compounds include a 1,3,5-triazine ring that is substituted at the 2-position with a methylthio group. Chloro-s-triazines, aromatic compounds possessing a 1,3,5-triazine ring that is substituted at the 2-position with a chlorine atom are a type of chemical moiety found in both CYN and TERB. However, the PY imprinted cavity recognition sites are not similar enough to these species which therefore induced less significant fluorescence quenching.

When compared to these analogues, melanin@N-GQDs@NIP did not demonstrate any substantial selectivity for PY. As a result, melanin@N-GQDs@NIP did not behave differently towards prometryn or its analogues. In this case, it is probable that the imprinted recognition sites played a crucial role because they were the exact same size, shape and location as the template. Competitive binding experiments were also carried out by maintaining the concentration of PY while increasing the concentration of TBY to assess how resistant melanin@N-GQDs@MIP is to any interference from chosen analogues. The quenching amount shows little to no variation with a rise in the CTBY/CPY ratio, as illustrated in Fig. S5B. The obtained data suggested that the modular composite melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe was sensitive and selective for the detection of prometryn.

The emission maxima of the fluorescent-integrated melanin (melanin@N-GQDs@MIP) sensor probe occurred at 406 nm. Fluorescence stability in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.0 was used as a measure of the stability of the improved nanocomposite (Fig. S6A). The results indicated that the fluorescence of the modified material was stable during a 300 min period, ensuring the precision of the analytical measurement. Long term stability was also evaluated for 6 months and the results indicated the synthesized melanin@N-GQDs@MIP probe was stable for at least 6 months (Fig. S7A). The results also exhibited that the developed sensing probe was stable under a range of pH solutions between 4.0 and 8.0 (Fig. S7B). The fluorescence intensity decrease at higher pH may be due to the silica layers of the composite sensing probe that could be ionized leading to the structural obstruction of binding sites. Furthermore, five additional synthesis batches of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP were utilized to determine prometryn and their sensitivities assessed to evaluate the fabrication reproducibility. Detection performance was essentially unchanged after five separate synthetic runs, demonstrating excellent repeatability (RSD = 1.07 %) (Fig. S6B). Although the molecular recognition system and the fluorescence sensing system would be required to be assessed to some extent for the analysis process, the reproducibility experiment demonstrated the fluorescence sensor to have excellent reproducibility as the average fluorescence sensitivity demonstrated a low relative standard deviation (RSD).

The analytical performance of the developed method based on melanin@N-GQDs@MIP sensor for the quantificationof prometryn was compared to other currently analytical methods (Table 2 ) including gas chromatography–nitrogen chemiluminescence detection [3], electrochemiluminescence [4], gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [5], surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectra (SERs) [32], lateral flow immunochromatographic (ICG) strip [8], SPE-LC [9] and sweepingmicellar electrokinetic chromatography(sweeping-MEKC)[33]. Melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probe employed in the current method can rapidly quantify prometryn herbicides within a sample matrix, compared to existing analytical techniques that may require additional centrifugation stages or external magnets. In addition, the amounts of organic solvents and operation times required by the melanin@N-GQDs@MIP sensor approach were considerably less than those required by other reported methods. In addition, its LOD was significantly lower than the maximum residue limits (MRLs)specified by the EU. Based on all investigationsand discussions, it demonstrates that the developed melanin@N-GQDs@MIP sensor exhibited great sensitivity and selectivity, a rapid analysis time and strong reproducibility and applicability for the determination of trace prometryn in fresh and/or processed fruit and vegetable samples.

A new approach for the specific detection of prometryn based on the metal-free nanocomposite fluorescent probe melanin@N-GQDs@MIP was developed. The use of melanin@N-GQDs@MIP as eco-friendly nanoprobes have the merits of the unique quenching capabilities of fluorescence emission and the high selectivity of MIPs. The hierarchical composite probe was fabricated by integrating bio-inspired melanin nanoparticles and N-doped graphene quantum dots within molecularly imprinted polymers (melanin@N-GQDs@MIP). The excellent sensitivity and specificity of the developed nanoprobes facilitated the detection of trace amounts of prometryn in vegetable samples. The melanin@N-GQDs@MIP nano-fluorescence probes displayed particular selectivity to prometryn over other structural mimics with detection limits down to 1.00 µg/L. The designed hierarchical composite probe was deployed as an optosensor to determine prometryn in real samples and high reliability was observed, recoveries between 92.4 and 102.8 % with RSDs < 6 %. The analytical results of the optosensor and HPLC were in excellent agreement, but the optosensor had superior sensitivity, was more straightforward to use and required less solvent consumption in analytical process and less expensive equipment. The developed method can be adapted for ultra-sensitive detection of a range of organic chemicals by the usage of different template molecules and improving some parameters for the determination of target analytes.

Peerapong Chumkaeo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Frank Davis: Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Conceptualization. Opas Bunkoed: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Postdoctoral Fellowship from Prince of Songkla University. The authors acknowledge the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115247.
